context.description: A philosophy of consciousness passage uses a mirror exercise to show the paradox of trying to observe one's own thinking. It distinguishes introspection about desires, hopes, and images from genuine thinking about thought itself.
normalization_notes: Translated visible German prose into English control language.
No secure book title or chapter number/title was visible beyond the section heading.

rows.paragraph_text: A mirror exercise is presented as a starting point for the journey, because looking at your own eyes physically enacts the mind's attempt to observe itself.
rows.text_content: Looking into the mirror is a starting point for the journey because it physically enacts the mind's attempt to observe itself.

rows.paragraph_text: The passage develops a paradox of self-observation in which consciousness appears frozen when it tries to see itself as a singular unit.
rows.text_content: Consciousness appears frozen in a paradox when it tries to see itself as a singular unit.

rows.paragraph_text: Introspection is separated from thinking about thought: one can reflect on wishes, hopes, dislikes, dreams, and internal images, but not on how those states arise or sustain themselves.
rows.text_content: Introspection is distinct from thinking about thought itself.
